digest.intro: War widens, concrete self-heals, and a font defeats AI classifiers.

items.title: US-Iran War, Day 6: Hormuz, Bridges, and a Widening Regional Fire
items.blurb: US strikes are now targeting bridges on routes into Bandar Abbas — the port city that controls Hormuz access — while Iran has fired on Qatar, Kuwait, Jordan, and US positions in Bahrain, Oman, and Syria in its largest retaliatory wave since talks collapsed. The strikes are concentrated on Iran's poorest, most ethnically non-Persian regions: one man told CNN his cousin's wife died in Ahvaz, and the city was hit again the following night. The geography of what's being destroyed is not accidental — it's a map of leverage over the strait through which a fifth of the world's oil moves.

items.title: Keir Starmer Out, Andy Burnham In — UK Gets Its Seventh Leader in a Decade
items.blurb: Starmer's resignation makes him another entry in what is becoming a running joke about British political durability — seven prime ministers in ten years. Burnham, former Mayor of Greater Manchester, steps into a government already battered by the economic drag of the Iran conflict and oil spike. Meanwhile Ukraine fired Mykhailo Fedorov, the minister who built its entire drone warfare and digital resistance apparatus, adding a third political earthquake to a day that already included a Trump-aligned far-right candidate winning Colombia's presidential runoff.

items.title: Your Brain Briefly Listens to Two Conversations at Once — and EEG Can See It
items.blurb: When you shift attention between two simultaneous speakers, your auditory cortex doesn't simply switch — it runs both streams in parallel for a measurable overlap window, engaging the new target before releasing the old one. The EEG signature of this moment is a drop in alpha power, a known index of cognitive load, and the researchers used LLMs to model four different lexical-prediction strategies to figure out how the brain resets its language context mid-switch. The implication: selective attention is less like a toggle and more like a brief chord before the hand moves to a new key.

items.title: Some People Are Better Eavesdroppers — What's Different in Their Brains
items.blurb: The readable companion to the PLOS Biology paper above: individual differences in this dual-stream encoding ability are measurable, consistent, and reflect something structural about how people's auditory cortices handle competing signals. The study framing — participants wore EEG harnesses listening to two simultaneous TED Talks amid background noise — is elegant in its mundanity. Worth reading back-to-back with the primary paper; together they make a clean argument for attention as a spectrum rather than a switch.

items.title: Microsoft Comic Chat Is Open Source — and It's Weirder Than You Remember
items.blurb: The 1996 IRC client that turned live text conversations into auto-generated comic panels — choosing poses, expressions, and panel layouts in real time via an expert system — is now fully on GitHub. It's also, incidentally, the software that gave Comic Sans its first real home: Vincent Connare designed the font specifically because speech bubbles needed something that read as spoken rather than typed. What's genuinely interesting isn't the nostalgia but the underlying design question it was answering: how do you add emotional valence and non-verbal signal to text-only communication without video? That question is still open.

items.title: Decoy Font: A Typeface That Reads One Thing to Humans, Another to AI
items.blurb: Built on spatial frequency separation — thin high-frequency outlines carry the decoy text that AI reads when analyzing pixels up close, while a blurred low-frequency mass spells the real message, legible when you step back or squint — the font exploits the fact that most LLM vision pipelines process images at close zoom. It's a TTF you can actually install and type with, and it reliably fools GPT and Gemini. Less a privacy tool than a working proof that the gap between human and machine perception is still wide enough to drive a typeface through.

items.title: Marc Padeu: Cocoa Plantations as the Stage for Divine Events
items.blurb: Cameroon-born painter Marc Padeu sets Renaissance-structured religious compositions — Adoration of the Magi iconography, gift-bearers, luminous central figures — inside West African cocoa plantation landscapes, colliding the postcolonial agricultural everyday with the weight of devotional art history. The show is titled Memento Vivere, the inverse of memento mori: remember to live rather than remember you'll die. The paintings land because the tension isn't ironic — the settings feel genuinely sacred, not appropriated.

items.title: A 1,900-Year-Old Roman Latrine Reveals Concrete That Heals Its Own Cracks
items.blurb: Researchers went to Hadrian's Villa specifically to study the communal toilets — unrestored, untouched, analytically pristine — and found that Roman concrete's millennium-scale durability isn't just pozzolanic chemistry (volcanic ash + lime + water) but also carbonation: CO₂ diffusing into cracks reacts with calcium to form calcite, physically sealing the damage over decades. Modern concrete typically fails within a century; Roman infrastructure is still load-bearing after two thousand years, and the latrine turns out to be the cleanest laboratory for understanding why. Published in Science Advances July 8.

facts.fact: The US strikes on Iran are concentrated on Bandar Abbas — specifically the bridges connecting to it — because the city sits on the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of global oil supply passes.

Comic Sans was designed by Microsoft typographer Vincent Connare specifically for Comic Chat's speech bubbles in 1994, making a latrine-level IRC client the unlikely birthplace of the world's most derided font.

During an auditory attention switch, the brain's EEG shows a drop in alpha power at the exact moment it briefly encodes two competing speech streams simultaneously — the new target comes online before the old one drops off.

Decoy Font uses separate spatial frequencies in a single letterform: AI classifiers reading pixels close-up see the decoy letter, while humans viewing the same character from normal reading distance see the real message.

Roman concrete's self-healing mechanism works via carbonation — CO₂ slowly diffuses into hairline cracks and reacts with calcium to deposit calcite, a process researchers confirmed by studying unrestored communal latrines at Hadrian's Villa.

make_something.prompt: Build a small spatial-frequency compositor: take any short sentence, render it twice — once as sharp high-frequency outlines (the decoy), once as a soft low-frequency blur (the real message) — and overlay them into a single image. Test whether a human reading at arm's length sees the blurred layer while a screenshot fed to an LLM reads the outline layer. You don't need the font; you can fake the effect in a canvas shader or even Photoshop blend modes. The interesting question is how far you can push the legibility gap before both layers collapse into noise.

art_picks.title: Zidane, a 21st Century Portrait
art_picks.creator: Douglas Gordon & Philippe Parreno
art_picks.why: Seventeen synchronized cameras locked on a single player for ninety minutes — never following the ball — making celebrity, obsession, and the structure of attention the real subject. It's Warhol's real-time portrait films crossed with the grammar of sports media, and it holds up as one of the most formally rigorous moving-image works of the 2000s.

art_picks.title: Fetch the Bolt Cutters
art_picks.creator: Fiona Apple
art_picks.why: An album built entirely around the acoustics of her house — objects, floors, dogs, bodies — where the recording environment becomes the instrument and the system becomes the statement. The obsessive, years-long construction of a world with its own physics; it sounds like nothing else because it refuses every external reference point.

art_picks.title: Untitled (Perfect Lovers)
art_picks.creator: Félix González-Torres
art_picks.why: Two identical clocks running in sync until entropy slowly desynchronizes them — a piece about love, mortality, and systems decay that lives entirely in the gap between concept and feeling. It's the rare work where the idea and the experience are inseparable, and where the 'object' is mostly duration.

events.name: In a Free State — curated by Salman Toor & Doron Langberg
events.venue: Luhring Augustine Tribeca, 17 White Street, Manhattan
events.why: Twenty-two artists across painting, sculpture, and moving image, assembled by two artists who drew from close circles and distant admirers to visualize a moment when 'the structures of trust have collapsed' — the kind of artist-curated group show where the premise actually earns its ambition.

events.name: Zidane, a 21st Century Portrait — Last Days
events.venue: Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum, 1071 Fifth Ave., Manhattan
events.why: The Gordon/Parreno film installation — 17 cameras fixed on one man, never the ball — closes Sunday; it's a meditation on spectatorship and celebrity running on loop in the Guggenheim's theater, and this is the final weekend.

events.name: MoMA PS1 Warm Up 2026 — Opening Night
events.venue: MoMA PS1, 22-25 Jackson Ave., Long Island City, Queens
events.why: Opening night with Carlos Souffront, a Wackies Showcase pairing Lloyd 'Bullwackie' Barnes with Basic Channel's Mark Ernestus (Bronx reggae dub meeting Berlin dub techno), and TELESONIQ — the most musically serious DJ party in New York, held in an art museum courtyard, in its 28th season.

events.name: SummerStage: UBB 40th Anniversary — BreakBeat Lou, Lord Finesse, Diamond D, Grand Puba & more
events.why: A free celebration of the 40th anniversary of the Ultimate Breaks & Beats series — the original sample-clearance-free vinyl that seeded hip-hop's entire sonic DNA — with the actual architects in the room; the kind of event where the history is the performance.

events.name: MoMA PS1 Warm Up 2026 — July 31
events.venue: MoMA PS1, 22-25 Jackson Ave., Long Island City, Queens
events.why: BAE BAE b2b Crystallmess, Gabber Eleganza, Cortisa Star, and SCRAAATCH (paying tribute to the late Yasunao Tone's Solo for Wounded CD with a noisy experimental set) — the most experimentally adventurous night of the summer series.
